summary: Gruber pays tribute to legendary baseball broadcaster Vin Scully, who died at 94 after 67 years calling Dodger games. He argues there is no serious debate that Scully was the greatest sports announcer ever, cataloging iconic moments Scully called including Hank Aaron's 715th home run, Kirk Gibson's 1988 World Series homer, and Sandy Koufax's perfect game. Gruber highlights Scully's unique gift for finding the perfect words in the moment, his mastery of knowing when to speak and when to let silence carry the drama. The piece is deeply personal, recalling how Scully called the first sports game that broke Gruber's heart as a child.
key_points: Scully's supremacy as a sports announcer is not a matter of debate — he was universally recognized as the best ever by peers like Bob Costas
Scully's genius lay in his ability to produce literary-quality prose spontaneously during live broadcasts, with transcripts that read like essays
His mastery included knowing when not to speak — the famous 70 seconds of silence during Gibson's home run let the moment belong to the fans
Scully's call of Hank Aaron's 715th home run showed his ability to elevate a sports moment into social commentary
His 67-year tenure with one franchise (1950-2016) represents an almost incomprehensible dedication to craft
money_quotes: There is no serious debate that Scully was not, hands-down, the best baseball announcer ever.
He was the broadcaster's broadcaster. The more one knows about how difficult sportscasting is, the more one stands in awe of Vin Scully.
In a year that has been so improbable, the impossible has happened.
You could sit with pen and paper for a year and not come up with better words. Scully came up with them on the spot, every time.
Transcripts of his calls read like literary essays.
His joy for the game was as palpable as it was contagious.
A lot of people in the ballpark now are starting to see the pitches with their hearts.

And then, for 70 seconds, as Gibson hobbled triumphantly around the bases, as his teammates celebrated at home plate, as the full house at Dodger Stadium erupted in ecstatic pandemonium, Scully said not a word.
key_insight: Vin Scully's genius was his ability to produce literary-quality prose in real time and to know when silence itself was the perfect call.
